pages.text: Effects of the usage of diatomite and waste marble powder as partial replacement of cement on the mechanical properties of concrete Ali Ergün * Afyon Kocatepe University, Technical Education Faculty, Construction Department, 03200 Afyonkarahisar, Turkey article info Article history: Received 29 December 2009 Received in revised form 7 July 2010 Accepted 18 July 2010 Available online 17 August 2010 Keywords: Diatomite Marble powder Cement replacement Compressive strength Flexural strength abstract Diatomite is a pozzolanic material containing amorphous silica, cristabolite and minor amounts of resid- ual minerals. Waste marble powder (WMP) is an inert material which is obtained as an industrial by product during sawing, shaping, and polishing of marble and causes a serious environmental problem. This paper describes the procedures and results of a laboratory investigation of mechanical properties carried out on the concrete specimens containing diatomite and WMP as partial replacement of cement in concrete. The laboratory work essentially consists of characterization of the raw and waste materials, preparation of concrete specimens with diatomite and WPM in different ratios by weight as replacement for cement and a superplasticizing admixture to reduce water demand and compression and ﬂexural tests of the specimens. Test results indicated that the concrete specimens containing 10% diatomite, 5% WPM and 5% WPM +10% diatomite replacement by weight for cement had the best compressive and ﬂexural strength and the replacement of cement with diatomite and WMP separately and together using a superplasticizing admixture could be utilized to improve the mechanical properties of the con- ventional concrete mixtures. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The most important mechanical properties of concrete are com- pressive and ﬂexural strength. The mechanical properties of con- crete can be improved with alternative materials used for partial replacement of Portland cement in concrete. These materials are widely utilized around the world with the advent of new European Standards [1], and are becoming more and more common due to some technical, economic and environmental reasons. One of these materials is diatomaceous rocks. Several large diatomaceous deposits have been discovered in Afyonkarahisar – Turkey [2]. Diatomite is a sedimentary rock of biogenic origin with high natu- ral amorphous silica content. The amorphous silica (opal-A) is mainly in the form of diatom frustules, and secondarily in the form of sponge spicules, silicone–ﬂagellate skeletons and/or radiolarian cells. This type of SiO 2 can react with Ca(OH) 2 and produce calcium silicate hydrates (CSH), which are responsible for the development of strength. Beside opal-A, the diatomite rocks commonly contain carbonate and clay minerals, quartz, feldspars and volcanic glass [3]. Diatomite is characterized as natural pozzolana, as it satisﬁes the requirements of EN 197-1 concerning the active silica content. Diatomite has high surface area because of its eased grindability. It can be used as a puzzolanic material for partial replacement of ce- ment in production of concrete. The effects of diatomite as additive on the properties of concrete have been studied intensively in recent years. Some authors have shown that the usage of diatomite in place of cement for produc- tion of cement mortar and concrete did not improve the mechan- ical properties [4,5]. But the othe

recent years. The presence of limestone in hardened cement paste has a ﬁller effect. Limestone is an inert or quasi-inert material being non-cementitious from hydraulic points of view [11,12]. The effects of limestone on cement properties are not only physical corresponding to reduction in paste porosity but also chemical. The chemical interactions take place between calcite and Portland ce- ment paste leading to calcium carboaluminates formed by a reac- tion between hydrated calcium aluminate and carbonate ions. Calcareous ﬁller has an important binding property that is devel- oped by hydration of calcite and C 3A [13]. The higher the amount of WMP additive the longer the setting times and the lower the strength of the specimens for all the curing periods [14]. The use of marble powder as mineral addition for mortars and concretes, especially for self compacting-concrete in the presence of a superplasticizing admixture provided maximum compressive strength at the same workability level, comparable to that of the reference mixture after 28 days of curing [15,16]. Binici et al. [17] investigated some mechanical properties of concrete contain- ing marble dusts and limestone dusts. The test results showed that there were clear increases of compressive strength, abrasion resis- tance, and sodium sulfate resistance with the increasing amount of marble dust. Furthermore, the use of marble and granite waste aggregates for production of concrete were investigated and deter- mined that could be used to improve the mechanical properties, workability and chemical resistance of the conventional concrete mixtures [18]. Up to now, many researchers have been made on the usage of diatomite and WMP in mortars and concretes as puzzolanic and in- ert ﬁller materials. High water demand was needed in tests with mineral powder addition to supply requested workability level. Generally, these additions have not contributed positive effects on mechanical properties of mortars and concretes because of higher water demand. In this study, the effects of usage of diatomite and WMP as par- tial replacement of cement on the mechanical properties of con- crete mixtures were examined extensively. It is the aim of this study to optimize the amount of diatomite and WMP added sepa- rate and together as replacement for cement in production of con- crete with lower water demand due to economic and environmental reasons. Three different concrete test series (i.e., Series-I, II and III) were prepared by replacing diatomite, WMP and diatomite and WMP together for cement, respectively. 2. Experimental studies 2.1. Materials and chemicals In this study, commercial ordinary Portland cement type CEM I 42.5R, according to the European Standards EN-197/1, was used when preparing the concrete spec- imens. The cement used had a ﬁneness of 4375 cm 2/g. The targeted 28-days-com- pressive concrete strength was set at 48.5 MPa. The chemical analysis of PC is given in Table 1 . Four different types of limestone aggregates were used when casting each of the test specimens, namely: crushed stone III (12–22 mm), crushed stone II (6–12 mm), crushed sand stone (0–6 mm) and river sand (0–4 mm). A water-reducing admixture was added to the mixtures at the ratio of 1% of bin- der materials by weight. It was constituted of polycarboxylates based polymer and high range water-reducing superplasticizer that was a third generation superplast- icizer for concrete and mortar. Diatomite ore from a deposit

was performed on ground bulk samples with the XRF spectrometer. Chemical and XRD analysis of the diatomite used in this study were presented in Table 1 and Fig. 1 . WMP from a marble factory in Afyonkarahisar – Turkey was used as a source of limestone additive. The WMP samples were crushed, homogenized and mineralog- ical analyzed with the diffractometer. XRD patterns showed that calcite is the main crystalline mineral of the WMP. In addition, dolomite is also identiﬁed in very low concentration at 31.5/C1762h. Chemical analysis of WMP was performed on ground bulk samples with the XRF spectrometer. Chemical and XRD analysis of the WMP used in this study were presented in Table 1 and Fig. 2 . 2.2. Sample preparations and test methods In order to assess the effects of diatomite and WMP as partial replacement of cement on the behavior of concrete, a constant water/binder (cement + ﬁne addi- tion) ratio of 0.50 was used for all the test specimens. Three series of concrete spec- imens were cast. The variables in this research were diatomite and WMP content. 5%, 7.5% and 10% diatomite replacement for Series-I, 5%, 7.5% and 10% WMP replacement for Series-II and 5% and 10% diatomite and WMP together replacement for Series-III were used. The designations, proportions, and some properties of the concrete mixtures for this three series (i.e., Series-I, II and III) are given in Table 2 . The concrete specimens were termed as Control, D5, D7.5, D10, M5, M7.5, M10, D5M5, D10M5, D5M10 and D10M5. Notation demonstrates the type of ﬁne addition and the percentage of the replacement of PC by ﬁne addition respectively. A 60 dm 3 batch was prepared for each mixture. For each investigated mixture, six 100 /C2100 /C2100 cubes and six 100 /C2100 /C2300 mm beams were tested from each of the three series (i.e., Ser- ies-I, II and III). The cubes and beams specimens were tested to failure to study the variation of the compressive strength and ﬂexural strength, respectively. For each of the test series, the effect of ﬁne addition was investigated and the speci- mens without ﬁne addition were considered as control specimens. Preparation of the batch and casting of the concrete into molds were given in Fig. 3 a and b. After 24 h. the concrete specimens produced were removed from the molds and cured in lime saturated water at 20 ± 2 /C176C until the time of testing. Mechanical properties such as; 7, 28 and 90 days compressive and ﬂexural strength were determined. The strength tests of the specimens were conducted according to Turkish standard procedure TS EN 12390 [20]. One-point loading conﬁguration was used for ﬂexural strength test. The tests were carried out using a closed-loop servo-hydraulic dy- namic testing machine, with a capacity of 2000 kN ( Fig. 4 a and b). The specimens were tested under strain control procedures. A constant axial strain rate was used throughout the experiments. 3. Results and discussion The replacement content of the PC by diatomite and WMP sep- arately or together was changed from 5% to 10% by weight while aggregate, water and superplasticizing admixture quantities were kept constant. The compressive and ﬂexural strength of the Fig. 2. XRD patterns of the WMP. Table 2 Concrete mix proportions (kg/m 3). Materials (kg/m 3) Control Series-I Series-II Series-III D5 D7.5 D10 M5 M7.5 M10 D5M5 D10M5 D5M10 D10M10 PC 300 285 277.5 270 285 277.5 270 270 255 255 240 Diatomite 0 15 22.5 30 0 0 0 15 30 15 30 WMP 0 0 0 0 15 22.5 30 15 15 30 30 Water

concrete were determined at 7, 28 and 90 days, and the test results were presented in Table 3 . Comparisons of the compressive and ﬂexural strengths of concrete with diatomite and WMP at any age against those of the control specimens were given in Figs. 5 and 6 , respectively. 3.1. Effect of diatomite replacement Chemical properties of diatomite were compared with those of the PC in Table 1. Chemical analysis indicated that the PC and diat- omite have different compositions and are principally composed of calcia and silica, respectively. Since diatomite powder has high sur- face area, cements containing diatomite powder have also high surface area. As expected the compressive strength increased with age. The compressive strength development of concrete with diat- omite is related to the cement replacement level and curing age. It was observed that the replacement of PC with 10% diatomite sig- niﬁcantly increased the compressive strength in this study while addition of 10% diatomite did not alter the compressive strength of diatomite cements at any age [2]. When these values compared with the control concretes, it could be seen that the specimens containing diatomite had higher compressive strength than that of the control concrete. The rate of strength is dependent on the combination of clinker hydration and the pozzolanic activity of diatomite. The main factors that affect the contribution of diato- mite in strength are: (a) the ﬁller effect, (b) the dilution effect, (c) the pozzolanic reaction of diatomite with CH, and (d) using a water-reducing admixture. The ﬁneness and the amount of diato- mite improved the initial porosity of the mix due to the obstruction of pores as the ﬁller effect. The dilution effect leads to less hydrated compounds due to the less clinker content of the blended cement. The pozzolanic reaction between the amorphous silica of diatomite and the calcium hydroxide produced by the cement hydration reactions caused the concrete to be more homogeneous and dense. This type (amorphous silica) of SiO 2 reacted with Ca(OH) 2 and pro- duced calcium silicate hydrates (CSH), which were responsible for the development of strength. The concretes with diatomite de- manded more water than that of the PC. The increase in water de- mand was mainly attributed to the high ﬁneness, amount and low density of diatomite in this case as well, but especially to the shape of its frustules (empty, perforated sheaths) which, with their very high capillary suction pressure, drew mixing water into their Fig. 3. (a and b) A 60 dm 3 batch and casting of the concrete into molds. Fig. 4. (a and b) Testing setup for specimens. Table 3 Variations of mechanical properties of test specimens. Ergün / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 806–812 809

interiors. The usage of a superplasticizing admixture decreased water demand in the production of concrete. The relative strength was the ratio of the strength of the con- crete with diatomite or WMP to that of the control specimens at the same age. As seen in Fig. 5 , the relative compressive strength of the concretes (D5, D7.5, D10) containing diatomite ranged from 1.10 to 1.28 at the age of 7 days, 1.02 to 1.20 at the age of 28 days, and 1.03 to 1.26 at the age of 90 days. However, the relative ﬂex- ural strength of the concrete specimens with diatomite did not change considerably ( Fig. 6 ). The concretes containing 5% diato- mite showed the only 3% increase in the ﬂexural strength com- pared to the control concrete at the age of 28 days while there was 26% increase in the compressive strength under same condi- tions. The relative strength values of all specimens with diatomite at early ages were higher than that of the age after 28 days. It was thought that this effect attributed to the usage of a superplasticiz- ing admixture. Degirmenci and Yılmaz [5] investigated the sulfate resistance of the cement mortar containing diatomite. They found that the mor- tar containing 10% and 15% diatomite showed excellent durability to sulfate attack. Usage of diatomite up to 10% as a replacement of PC improved the mechanical properties of concretes. However, it is said that concretes with diatomite replacement have good perfor- mance on the durability, too. 3.2. Effect of WMP replacement Chemical properties of WMP were compared with those of the PC in Table 1 . Chemical analysis indicated that the main compo- nent of WMP is calcia. The amount of potassium oxide known as ‘‘alkali” was found lower in WMP (0.21%) than that of the PC (0.73%). WMP had higher surface area than PC because of the ﬁne- ness of WMP used. The 5% replacement of WMP with cement leads to an increase in the compressive strength. The increase in compressive strength could be explained with pore-ﬁlling effect of ﬁne-ground lime- stone powder, providing suitable nucleus for hydration and cata- lyzing the hydration as a result. The usage of WMP in concrete showed as a ﬁller effect. The reason could be said as that the ﬁller was an inert addition and it could be assumed as ultraﬁne aggre- gates ﬁlling voids in concrete. The usage of WMP reduced the porosity in concrete matrix physically, and had an important bind- ing property which was developed by hydration of calcite and C 3A chemically. The use of WMP in combination with superplasticizing 0.5 0.6 0.7 0.8 0.9 1 1.1 1.2 1.3 1.4 Control D5 D7.5 D10 M5 M7.5 M10 D5M5 D10M5 D5M10 D10M10 Relative Compressive Strength Series 7. day 28. day 90. day Fig. 5. Comparisons of the ﬂexural strength of concrete with diatomite and WMP at any age against control specimens. 810 A. Ergün / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 806–812

admixture allowed for compensating the high water demand of marble powder itself. The superplasticizing admixture contributed positive effects to the strength of low ratio WMP replacement specimens for decreasing water demand. As shown in Figs. 5 and 6 that there is a clear increase of compressive strength with increasing of diatomite replacement. On the contrary, the compres- sive strength decreased with the increasing WMP replacement. It was observed that M10 specimens had lower strength values than the control concrete specimens due to potential reduction in the cementing materials which was commonly known as the dilution of the pozzolanic reactions. The relative compressive strength of the concretes (M5, M7.5, M10) containing WMP ranged from 1.11 to 0.95 at the age of 7 days, 1.11 to 0.88 at the age of 28 days, and 1.12 to 0.86 at the age of 90 days ( Fig. 5 ). However, the relative ﬂexural strength of the concretes with WMP did not change considerably ( Fig. 6 ). There was only an increase in the ﬂexural strength of M5 speci- mens. The concretes containing 5% WMP showed the only 5% in- crease in the ﬂexural strength compared to the control concrete specimens at the age of 90 days while there was 12% increase in the compressive strength under same conditions. Similar increases were found by Binici et al. [17]. 3.3. Effect of diatomite and WMP replacement applied together Test results showed that up to a certain value of replacing ce- ment with either diatomite or WMP using a superplasticizing admixture in concrete matrix had high compressive strength. It was thought that concrete added both diatomite and WMP using a superplasticizing admixture had higher compressive strength. The relative compressive strength of the concretes (D5M5, D10M5, D5M10, D10M10) containing diatomite and WMP ranged from 1.08 to 1.30 at the age of 7 days, 1.06 to 1.19 at the age of 28 days, and 1.11 to 1.20 at the age of 90 days ( Fig. 5). The concrete specimens with the highest relative strength were 10% diatomite +5% WMP (D10M5) at the age of 7 days and it was 1.30 with re- spect to the control concrete specimens. However, the relative ﬂex- ural strength of the concretes with diatomite and WMP gradually increased ( Fig. 6 ). The D10M5 specimens showed 9% increase in the ﬂexural strength compared to the control concrete at the age of 90 days while there was 20% increase in the compressive strength under same conditions. The concrete specimens including the replacement of cement with the combination of 10% diatomite and 5% WMP showed the highest increase in compressive and ﬂex- ural strength at all ages. The rate of strength depended on the clin- ker hydration and pozzolanic activity of diatomite, ﬁller effect of WMP and water-reducing effect of a superplasticizing admixture. It can be observed from Figs. 5 and 6 that relative strength val- ues of all specimens containing diatomite were high at early ages. However, the relative strengths values of the group WMP concrete specimens were almost at the same level for all ages. This attrib- uted to the usage of a superplasticizing admixture which enhanced the early age strengths. 3.4. The coefﬁcient of variation (COV) values Fig. 7 indicates coefﬁcient of variation (COV) values of compres- sive strengths of specimens. It seems that the effects of diatomite and WMP replacement in different ratio on the COV values cannot be generalized. However, it is interesting that the COV values for concretes with diatomite repl

/C15The concrete containing 5% WMP as partial replacement by weight for cement with a superplasticizing admixture had higher compressive strength than that of the control concrete specimens. 5% WMP replacement improved the mechanical properties of concretes. The positive effect that causes the increase in compressive strength stems from ﬁller properties of WMP as in limestone powder and low water demand caused by adding superplasticizing admixture. /C15The concrete specimens containing 10% diatomite +5% WMP mutual replacement of PC (D10M5) had highest compressive and ﬂexural strength. The relative compressive strengths of this series (D10M5) with respect to control concrete specimens were 1.30 at the age of 7 days while the relative ﬂexural strength was 1.09 at the age of 90 days. The combination of puz- zolanic effect of diatomite, the ﬁller material effect of WMP and low water demand effect of superplasticizing admixture increased the compressive and ﬂexural strength of D10M5 series. /C15The coefﬁcient of variation (COV) values of concretes with diat- omite replacement and control specimens are approximately similar at all curing times. This is also an important conclusion for having good performance on the mechanical properties of concretes with diatomite replacement. /C15The results of this study showed that the replacement of cement with diatomite and WMP separately or together using a superplasticizing admixture could be used to improve the mechanical properties of the conventional concrete mixtures. Of the mechanical strengths, compressive strength of concrete is the main feature which allows assessment of concrete durability. Since diatomite and WMP are available in vast amounts in Turkey, it makes sense from the economical and environmental viewpoints to use these materials as cement replacement in the production of more durable concrete mixtures. According to the experimental results, it is suggested that diat- omite and WMP can be used up to 10% and 5% separately or to- gether as a replacement of PC in production of concrete, respectively. The superplasticizing admixture must be also used in these concrete mixtures. References [1] Baron J, Douvre C. Technical and economical aspects of the use of limestone ﬁller additions in cement.
